questions.question: What does the MNIST dataset contain?
questions.explanation: MNIST contains 70,000 grayscale images of handwritten digits (0-9), each 28x28 pixels.

questions.question: How many classes does the MNIST classification problem have?
questions.explanation: MNIST has 10 classes, one for each digit from 0 to 9.

questions.question: What type of classification problem is distinguishing digit 5 from all other digits?
questions.explanation: When distinguishing one class from all others (5-detector), it is a binary classification problem.

questions.question: Which Scikit-Learn estimator is commonly used for multiclass classification using the one-vs-rest strategy by default?
questions.explanation: SGDClassifier uses One-vs-Rest (OvR) by default for multiclass classification.

questions.question: What does a confusion matrix show?
options: The number of correct and incorrect predictions for each class
The learning curve of the model
questions.explanation: A confusion matrix shows the counts of true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives for each class.

questions.question: In a confusion matrix, what does a high number of off-diagonal elements indicate?
questions.explanation: Off-diagonal elements represent misclassifications, so a high number indicates many errors.

questions.question: What is the formula for accuracy in classification?
questions.explanation: Accuracy = (True Positives + True Negatives) / Total number of samples.

questions.question: Why is accuracy not always a good performance measure for classification?
options: It can be misleading with imbalanced datasets
It is too complex to compute
It only works for binary classification
questions.explanation: With imbalanced data (e.g., 95% negatives), a classifier predicting all negatives gets 95% accuracy but is useless.

questions.question: What is precision in classification?
options: The ratio of correctly predicted positives to all actual positives
The ratio of all predictions to correct predictions
The ratio of correctly predicted positives to all predicted positives
The ratio of false positives to true positives
questions.explanation: Precision = TP / (TP + FP). It measures the accuracy of positive predictions.

questions.question: What is recall (sensitivity) in classification?
options: The ratio of correctly predicted positives to all predicted positives
The ratio of true negatives to all actual negatives
The ratio of correctly predicted positives to all actual positives
The ratio of false negatives to true positives
questions.explanation: Recall = TP / (TP + FN). It measures the fraction of actual positives that were correctly identified.

questions.question: What does a high precision but low recall indicate?
options: The classifier misses many positives but is confident when it predicts positive
The classifier catches all positives but makes many false alarms
questions.explanation: High precision means few false positives. Low recall means many false negatives (missed positives).

questions.question: What is the F1 score?
options: The arithmetic mean of precision and recall
The geometric mean of precision and recall
The harmonic mean of precision and recall
The maximum of precision and recall
questions.explanation: F1 = 2 * (Precision * Recall) / (Precision + Recall). It is the harmonic mean, giving a balanced measure.

questions.question: Why is the harmonic mean used in F1 score instead of the arithmetic mean?
options: It punishes extreme values more severely
It is the standard in all statistics
questions.explanation: The harmonic mean gives much more weight to low values, so both precision and recall must be high to get a high F1.

questions.question: How can you adjust the precision/recall tradeoff in an SGDClassifier?
options: By increasing the number of iterations
questions.explanation: You can adjust the decision threshold. Raising it increases precision but decreases recall, and vice versa.

questions.question: What does the decision_function method in Scikit-Learn return?
options: The probability estimates for each class
A score for each instance indicating confidence
questions.explanation: decision_function returns a score for each instance. Instances with higher scores are more likely to be positive.

questions.question: What is the ROC curve?
options: A plot of precision vs recall
A plot of accuracy vs training size
A plot of True Positive Rate vs False Positive Rate
A plot of loss vs epochs
questions.explanation: ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) curve plots TPR (recall) against FPR at various threshold settings.

questions.question: What is the True Positive Rate (TPR) also known as?
questions.explanation: TPR = TP / (TP + FN), which is identical to recall or sensitivity.

questions.question: What is the False Positive Rate (FPR)?
questions.explanation: FPR = FP / (FP + TN). It is the ratio of negative instances incorrectly classified as positive.

questions.question: What does the Area Under the ROC Curve (ROC AUC) measure?
options: The average precision across all thresholds
The training time of the model
The ability to distinguish between positive and negative classes
questions.explanation: ROC AUC measures the classifier's ability to distinguish between classes. A perfect classifier has AUC = 1.

questions.question: What ROC AUC score would a purely random classifier achieve?
questions.explanation: A random classifier produces an ROC curve along the diagonal, giving an AUC of 0.5.

questions.question: When should you use Precision-Recall curves instead of ROC curves?
options: When you have a balanced dataset
When you want to compare multiple models
When you have an imbalanced dataset with few positives
When you have a binary classification problem
questions.explanation: PR curves are more informative than ROC curves for imbalanced datasets with few positive instances.

questions.question: What is One-vs-Rest (OvR) strategy in multiclass classification?
options: Training one classifier per class, distinguishing that class from all others
Training one classifier per pair of classes
Training a single classifier for all classes simultaneously
Training classifiers in a hierarchical tree
questions.explanation: OvR trains N classifiers for N classes. Each classifier distinguishes one class from all others.

questions.question: What is One-vs-One (OvO) strategy in multiclass classification?
options: Training one classifier per class against all others
Training a single classifier with multiple outputs
Training one classifier per pair of classes
questions.explanation: OvO trains N*(N-1)/2 classifiers, one for each pair of classes. It is preferred by some algorithms like SVM.

questions.question: Which Scikit-Learn classifier uses One-vs-One by default for multiclass problems?
questions.explanation: SVC (Support Vector Classifier) uses OvO by default because it scales poorly with large datasets.

questions.question: What is multilabel classification?
options: Classifying into multiple mutually exclusive classes
Assigning multiple labels to each instance
questions.explanation: In multilabel classification, each instance can belong to multiple classes simultaneously (e.g., tagging a photo with multiple labels).

questions.question: Which Scikit-Learn wrapper can convert a binary classifier into a multilabel classifier?
questions.explanation: MultiOutputClassifier trains one classifier per output label, enabling multilabel classification.

questions.question: What is multioutput classification?
options: Using multiple models for a single prediction
Predicting multiple labels per instance where each label can have multiple classes
questions.explanation: Multioutput classification is a generalization where each output can be multiclass (e.g., predicting pixel colors in an image).

questions.question: What is error analysis in classification?
options: Analyzing the types of mistakes the model makes
questions.explanation: Error analysis involves examining the confusion matrix to understand which classes are confused with each other.

questions.question: What is a common preprocessing step for image classification with Scikit-Learn?
options: Using data augmentation by rotating images 360 degrees
questions.explanation: Scikit-Learn classifiers expect flat feature vectors, so 28x28 images are typically reshaped into 784-dimensional vectors.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a validation set in classification?
options: To increase the training data size
To tune hyperparameters and select models without touching the test set
questions.explanation: A validation set is used for model selection and hyperparameter tuning to avoid overfitting to the test set.

questions.question: What does the cross_val_score function in Scikit-Learn do?
options: Trains a single model on all data
Performs k-fold cross-validation and returns evaluation scores
questions.explanation: cross_val_score splits data into k folds, trains on k-1 folds, validates on the remaining fold, and returns all scores.

questions.question: What is k-fold cross-validation?
options: Training k different models on the same data
Repeating training k times with different random seeds
Using k nearest neighbors for classification
Splitting data into k parts and using each part as validation once
questions.explanation: In k-fold CV, the data is split into k subsets. Each subset serves as validation once while the rest is used for training.

questions.question: What is stratified k-fold cross-validation?
options: Using random splits for each fold
Preserving the class proportions in each fold
questions.explanation: Stratified k-fold ensures each fold has approximately the same class distribution as the full dataset.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of cross-validation over a single train-test split?
options: It automatically selects the best model
It provides a more reliable estimate of model performance
questions.explanation: Cross-validation averages performance across multiple splits, reducing the variance of the performance estimate.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the classification_report function in Scikit-Learn?
options: To generate a confusion matrix plot
To display precision, recall, F1-score, and support for each class
questions.explanation: classification_report provides a detailed summary of precision, recall, F1-score, and support per class.

questions.question: What is the support metric in a classification report?
options: The number of actual occurrences of each class in the dataset
questions.explanation: Support is the number of true instances for each class in the test set.

questions.question: What is the macro average in a classification report?
options: The unweighted mean of per-class metrics
The average across all training folds
questions.explanation: Macro average computes the unweighted mean of per-class metrics, treating all classes equally regardless of size.

questions.question: What is the weighted average in a classification report?
options: The unweighted mean of per-class metrics
The average weighted by the number of true instances per class
The average of the top-performing classes
questions.explanation: Weighted average accounts for class imbalance by weighting each class's metric by its support.

questions.question: In binary classification, what is a False Positive?
options: A negative instance correctly classified as negative
A negative instance incorrectly classified as positive
A positive instance incorrectly classified as negative
A positive instance correctly classified as positive
questions.explanation: False Positive (Type I error): a negative instance that the classifier incorrectly labels as positive.

questions.question: In binary classification, what is a False Negative?
options: A negative instance incorrectly classified as positive
A positive instance incorrectly classified as negative
questions.explanation: False Negative (Type II error): a positive instance that the classifier misses and labels as negative.

questions.question: What is specificity in classification?
questions.explanation: Specificity = TN / (TN + FP). It measures the proportion of actual negatives correctly identified.

questions.question: What happens to precision if you increase the decision threshold?
questions.explanation: Increasing the threshold makes the classifier more conservative, reducing false positives and thus increasing precision.

questions.question: What happens to recall if you increase the decision threshold?
questions.explanation: A higher threshold means fewer instances are classified as positive, leading to more false negatives and lower recall.

questions.question: What is the baseline classifier for comparing classification performance?
options: A classifier that always predicts the most frequent class
A classifier that predicts all instances as positive
questions.explanation: The most-frequent-class classifier serves as a simple baseline to ensure your model actually learns something useful.

questions.question: What does it mean if a classifier's ROC curve hugs the top-left corner?
options: The classifier performs no better than random
The classifier needs more training data
questions.explanation: A curve near the top-left indicates high TPR and low FPR across thresholds, meaning excellent classification performance.

questions.question: Can you use SGDClassifier for multiclass classification directly?
options: No, it only supports binary classification
Yes, Scikit-Learn automatically uses OvR strategy
Yes, but only with OvO strategy
questions.explanation: Scikit-Learn automatically runs SGDClassifier multiple times under the hood using the OvR strategy for multiclass.

questions.question: What is the primary goal when choosing a decision threshold?
options: To balance precision and recall based on business needs
To maximize the number of features
questions.explanation: The optimal threshold depends on the cost of false positives vs false negatives in your specific application.

questions.question: What type of problem is spam detection typically?
questions.explanation: Spam detection is binary: an email is either spam or not spam (ham).

questions.question: What is the key difference between multiclass and multilabel classification?
options: In multiclass, each instance belongs to exactly one class; in multilabel, instances can belong to multiple classes
Multilabel is only for text data
questions.explanation: Multiclass: one label per instance. Multilabel: multiple labels can apply to a single instance simultaneously.

questions.question: Which metric is most appropriate when false negatives are very costly (e.g., disease detection)?
questions.explanation: Recall is crucial when missing positive cases is costly, as it measures the ability to find all actual positives.
